name: Horse Mt (West Side)
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding in the Six Rivers National Forest area. Collect quartz, chalcedony, and jasper specimens from moderate slopes at 4,380 feet elevation.
description:
Horse Mountain's west side offers accessible rockhounding opportunities on public BLM land within the Six Rivers National Forest boundary in northern Humboldt County, California. The moderate slopes and mixed conifer forest setting create a scenic collecting environment where you can search for quartz veins, chalcedony specimens, and jasper fragments scattered across the terrain. This location sits within the Klamath Mountains province, where Paleozoic metamorphic rocks and Mesozoic intrusions created abundant silica-rich mineral deposits that weather out along hillsides and in small seasonal drainages.

The elevation of 4,380 feet places this site in the transitional forest zone where Douglas fir, tanoak, and madrone mix with understory brush. Rockhounders typically find the best specimens on exposed ridgelines and along weathered outcrop areas where natural erosion has exposed fresh material. The moderate slope means walking is steady but not exhausting, and parking pullouts provide reasonable access to the main collecting areas.

Collecting here follows standard BLM personal use guidelines: take only what you can carry, respect the 25-pound daily limit, and avoid disturbing any mining claims or protected features. The area sees light to moderate use, so you may have solitude but should always check in with local BLM offices for any seasonal closures. Many collectors combine trips here with adjacent national forest areas for a full day of exploration.
history: This region sits within the Klamath Mountains geologic province where Paleozoic metamorphic basement rocks were intruded by Mesozoic granitic batholiths. The resulting hydrothermal alteration and weathering of quartz-rich intrusions created abundant secondary deposits of quartz, chalcedony, and silicate minerals that concentrate along hillslopes and drainage systems at elevations between 3,000 and 5,500 feet.
tips: Search along the western exposure of Horse Mountain where erosion has naturally exposed quartz veins and weathered rock outcrops; focus on areas where the slope flattens slightly and drainage patterns converge. Look for white to gray quartz specimens and banded chalcedony fragments in the soil and among loose rocks on the steeper slopes. Bring a sturdy rock hammer or chisel to test embedded specimens in outcrop, and check smaller ravines where seasonal water flow concentrates heavier minerals. Early morning light on east-facing slopes helps you spot translucent quartz specimens that can otherwise blend into the surrounding granite.
terrain_notes: Moderate slope terrain at 4,380 feet elevation with mixed conifer forest cover. Walking distance from parking area to main collecting zones is typically one-quarter to one-half mile. Footing is uneven with rocky outcrops, roots, and loose material underfoot. Vegetation includes Douglas fir, tanoak, madrone, and chaparral understory. Some areas have exposed bedrock and weathered granite, while others are covered in needle duff and organic soil. Sturdy hiking boots are essential for safe travel.
best_season: May - October. The higher elevation (4,380 ft) means spring snow melt clears by late May. Summer and early fall offer dry conditions and stable footing. Winter and early spring bring heavy rain to the northern California coast range, making trails muddy and hazardous. October offers ideal conditions with cooler temperatures and minimal mud.
written_directions: From Scotia or Rio Dell in southern Humboldt County, take Highway 101 north toward Fortuna. Exit toward Alderpoint and head east on Alderpoint Road. Continue approximately 15 miles on roads that become increasingly unpaved and narrow as you approach the Six Rivers National Forest boundary. Look for Forest Service markers and informal pullouts on the west side of Horse Mountain. GPS navigation is strongly recommended as signage is minimal. The final 3-4 miles are rough dirt road passable in dry conditions with a high-clearance vehicle.
road_conditions: Rough unpaved forest service roads with washboard and ruts. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Impassable in wet conditions or winter. Final approach is steep and narrow in places.
parking_notes: Limited informal pullouts along forest service roads on western approaches. Space for 2-4 vehicles. No developed parking lot or facilities. Pullouts are unsigned; look for established turnouts near trail markers.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or blasting. Respect any marked mining claims. Leave all archaeological and paleontological features undisturbed. Pack out all trash.
rules:

nearest_services: Scotia and Rio Dell, approximately 25-30 miles west via Highway 101. Limited services; closest significant town is Fortuna (35 miles southwest) with gas, food, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with moderate slope and dense forest cover. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required due to loose rocks, roots, and uneven ground. The 0.25 to 0.5 mile walk from parking to best collecting zones is steady climbing but not technically difficult for most ages. Be prepared for temperature swings; higher elevation can be cool even in summer.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Horse Mt west side in Humboldt County?
faq.answer: The primary specimens are quartz, chalcedony, agate, and jasper. Quartz veins weather out along the hillslopes, while chalcedony and agate fragments are scattered in soil and drainage areas. Most collectors find white to gray quartz and banded chalcedony within 100 feet of exposed rock outcrops on the western slope.

faq.question: Is there a permit required for rockhounding at Horse Mt west side, and what are the rules?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on this BLM public land. You may collect up to 25 pounds per day for personal use only. Commercial collection, motorized excavation, and blasting are prohibited. Always respect marked mining claims and leave archaeological features undisturbed.

faq.question: What is the best season and weather to rockhound Horse Mt in northern California?
faq.answer: May through October is ideal. The high elevation (4,380 ft) clears snow by late May. Summer and early fall offer dry, stable footing. Winter and spring bring heavy rain to the coast range, making trails muddy and hazardous. October is peak season with cool temperatures and minimal mud.

faq.question: How do I get to Horse Mt west side rockhounding area from the nearest town?
faq.answer: From Rio Dell or Scotia, take Highway 101 north toward Fortuna, then exit toward Alderpoint Road heading east. Continue approximately 15 miles on unpaved forest service roads into the Six Rivers National Forest. High-clearance or AWD vehicle is recommended due to rough, washboard conditions. GPS navigation is strongly advised.

faq.question: Is Horse Mt west side rockhounding suitable for beginners and families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, this location is beginner-friendly with moderate difficulty. Children ages 8 and up can participate in the collecting. The walk from parking is less than one-half mile with steady but not steep terrain. Dogs are welcome on leash. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and sturdy hiking boots for rocky terrain.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding quartz and chalcedony at Horse Mt?
faq.answer: Bring a small rock hammer or chisel to test embedded specimens in outcrop areas. A heavy backpack or collection bag is essential to carry specimens up to the 25-pound daily limit. Wear sturdy hiking boots with good grip for uneven forest terrain. Bring a map, compass or GPS device, plenty of water, sun protection, and a first aid kit. The area has spotty cell service, so plan accordingly.

meta_title: Horse Mt West Side Rockhounding - California
meta_description: Find quartz, chalcedony, and agate on public BLM land near Rio Dell, California. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Best May - October.
